patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the memcontrol code where NMI slab stats are not propagated to memcg vmstats, leading to inconsistent statistics. The fix involves modifying the flush_nmi_stats function to update both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 10:28:19AM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> flush_nmi_stats() drains per-node NMI slab atomics into the per-node
> lruvec_stats, but does not propagate them to the memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> For non NMI case, account_slab_nmi_safe() calls mod_memcg_lruvec_state()
> which updates both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, so
> flush_nmi_stats() needs to flush to per-node lruvec_stats as well as
> memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> So fix this by flushing to the memcg-level vmstats for NMI too.
> 
> Fixes: 940b01fc8dc1 ("memcg: nmi safe memcg stats for specific archs")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Harry Yoo
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and added a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/18/26 5:28 PM, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> flush_nmi_stats() drains per-node NMI slab atomics into the per-node
> lruvec_stats, but does not propagate them to the memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> For non NMI case, account_slab_nmi_safe() calls mod_memcg_lruvec_state()
> which updates both per-node lruvec_stats and memcg-level vmstats, so
> flush_nmi_stats() needs to flush to per-node lruvec_stats as well as
> memcg-level vmstats.
> 
> So fix this by flushing to the memcg-level vmstats for NMI too.
> 
> Fixes: 940b01fc8dc1 ("memcg: nmi safe memcg stats for specific archs")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---

Looks good to me,
Reviewed-by: Harry Yoo (Oracle) <harry@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to detect and prevent various race conditions that can occur when deleting a qgroup (quota group) while writing quotas. The tests cover scenarios where an extent's owner reference outlives its subvolume, leading to potential underflows or transaction aborts. The patch aims to ensure correct quota accounting by rejecting qgroup deletion requests in these situations.

review_comments.author: Anand Jain
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the transaction commit could race with the testcase, and suggested checking for kernel fixes XXXXXXXXXXXX and 0c309d66dacd to resolve the issue.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a test failure due to an unfixed kernel issue, but did not commit to revising the patch.

Author Boris Burkov responded to a question about which kernel branch the reviewer was running on and whether they had applied a specific patch (404bf5a24f2e) that fixes a critical issue related to squota qgroup deletion. He provided evidence from git logs showing that this patch is not present in the 7.1-rc3 tag but is included in the btrfs/for-next branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for running the test and reporting the failure, sorry for the
inconvenience.

---

What kernel branch are you running this on? I couldn't find the sha
f5d9f327e4b2, but I took a look at linus's 7.1-rc3 tag and the fixes
aren't there:

$ git ql v7.1-rc3 fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
e0a85137a882 btrfs: avoid GFP_ATOMIC allocations in qgroup free paths
9a0448847310 btrfs: unexport btrfs_qgroup_reserve_meta()
534c0adacdeb btrfs: collapse __btrfs_qgroup_reserve_meta() into btrfs_qgroup_reserve_meta_prealloc()
5adf3f32c0b3 btrfs: collapse __btrfs_qgroup_free_meta() into btrfs_qgroup_free_meta_prealloc()

vs.

$ git ql btrfs/for-next fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
5c3eb7a1bce0 btrfs: swallow btrfs_record_squota_delta() ENOENT
5e4ccd2a4e70 btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow
ed2bb38054d9 btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation
404bf5a24f2e btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup
452a1a22e98b btrfs: add missing unlikely to if branches leading to a DEBUG_WARN()
e0a85137a882 btrfs: avoid GFP_ATOMIC allocations in qgroup free paths
9a0448847310 btrfs: unexport btrfs_qgroup_reserve_meta()

I believe that:
404bf5a24f2e btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup

is the critical one, as mentioned in the test's "You _MAY_ be missing
kernel fix" section. Do you have that patch?

If not, I think that is the most likely explanation.

Thanks,
Boris

Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <asj@kernel.org>

A small nit (which can be fixed during merge).

---

Thanks.

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: inline enable_quota helper in test 301

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the btrfs test suite by inlining two calls to enable quota functionality, eliminating a helper function and reducing unnecessary complexity.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where non-reclaimable memory allocations, such as those made under network-heavy workloads, fail when defrag_mode is enabled due to the inability to enforce ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT. The patch clears ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retries the allocation before giving up, following the same pattern used after reclaim/compaction exhaustion. This fix addresses a large number of SLUB allocation failures for skbuff_head_cache under network-heavy workloads, despite free memory being available in other migratetype freelists.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether the patch's change could potentially cause a violation of the rule that 'No "goto retry;" can be placed above this check * unless it can execute just once', and expressed uncertainty about another comment from Sashiko
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks.  Sashiko asked a couple of things:

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260518163736.173910-1-d@ilvokhin.com

I'm not sure what to make of the first one - we aren't holding any locks
in there which prevent concurrent cpuset or zonelist alterations
anyway (?).

But your change might violate the later comment `No "goto retry;" can be
placed above this check * unless it can execute just once'?

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addresses a concern about the behavior of defrag_mode when allocations cannot directly reclaim memory. They explain that the goto retry label can only execute once and ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is cleared before it, so on subsequent iterations, the condition is false and the allocation fails prematurely. The author also clarifies that for PF_MEMALLOC checks, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT has already been cleared, but for GFP_NORETRY allocations, direct reclaim is possible and another round is not needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look, Andrew.

Goto retry can execute at most once, since ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is cleared
before the jump, so on the next iteration the condition is false and we
fall through to goto nopage. This is the similar to the existing
can_retry_reserves path.

Just for the sake of keeping everything in one place. Another point
Sashiko raised.

"Will allocations hitting this PF_MEMALLOC check, or the __GFP_NORETRY check
further down in the function, still fail prematurely under defrag_mode=1?
Because these terminal error paths also jump directly to the nopage label,
they skip the normal ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT clearing at the bottom of the slowpath.
Should we also clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry for these paths so they are
allowed to fall back rather than failing outright?"

I think by the time we reach the PF_MEMALLOC check, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT has
already been cleared, since we set only ALLOC_NO_WATERMARKS and
ALLOC_KSWAPD in reserve_flags, when PF_MEMALLOC is set.

For GFP_NORETRY, we can do direct reclaim (compared to GFP_ATOMIC case),
so we either succeed or not, we don't need another round.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch's change is sufficient for non-reclaimable allocations and there's no need to re-evaluate the cpuset, deeming it acceptable

The reviewer pointed out that the patch loses the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag for privileged requests due to alloc_flags being overwritten, and suggested this is a problem because it causes a retry with the flag already cleared.

Reviewer noted that defrag_mode's strategy of enforcing ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT may not be suitable for GFP_ATOMIC allocations, as failing is an OOM-like event and the allocator should allow fallbacks instead. However, they suggested adding a check to see if __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM is set before deciding between fragmenting and failing.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, it's just a one-off retry with relaxed fragmentation rules, no
need to re-evaluate the cpuset. So this looks fine to me.

---

Yes, that's correct. alloc_flags gets overwritten, losing NOFRAGMENT,
for privileged requests. And then we retry with that already.

---

This is an interesting question.

GFP_NORETRY can reclaim and compact once, yes, but ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is
still a higher bar, increasing the likelihood of failure.

However, unlike GFP_ATOMIC, GFP_NORETRY are usually speculative
allocations with reasonable fallback options (like slub's optimistic
higher order requests).

The idea behind defrag_mode is to not fragment until the alternative
is OOM. For GFP_ATOMIC, failing is an OOM-like event. For the other
nopage cases, it's more about "my favorite thing isn't available".

So I'd say let's fix GFP_ATOMIC and leave the other cases alone unless
somebody specifically brings it up as an issue.

However, there is one catch: GFP_ATOMIC is not its own flag. You're
gating on can_direct_reclaim which is also true for optimistic things
like mTHP allocations (GFP_TRANSHUGE_LIGHT e.g.). We don't want to
fragment for those, either.

So I think you'd want to check at least if __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM is set
(which it is for GFP_ATOMIC) to decide between fragmenting and
failing. If the caller doesn't even set that, it's a good indicator
that they're purely speculative, and failing is the better option.

With that,

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards
[PATCH v3 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to remove dead NULL checks from unconditional guard destructors in the Linux kernel. The patches prepare guards that handle NULL, add __nonnull() attributes to constructors for compile-time enforcement, and finally remove the unnecessary NULL checks.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Identified that the __nonnull() attribute does not prevent NULL pointers from being passed, but rather serves as a best-effort compiler warning. Suggested updating links to GCC documentation and rewording the commit message.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 5:22 PM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> Add __nonnull() to unconditional guard constructors so the compiler
> verifies at each call site that NULL is never passed:

> This provides automated, compiler-enforced verification that no
> unconditional guard constructor receives NULL.

I wouldn't say "verify", since the compiler does a best-effort here
with the information it has statically.

In other words, the attribute does not prevent NULL pointers to be passed.

> + *   gcc: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-nonnull-function-attribute

Hmm... It appears GCC has changed the docs in commit 6e3c137f5dbb
("doc: Merge function, variable, type, and statement attribute
sections [PR88472]"), dropping the per-kind attribute pages.

So the right link would need to be now:

  https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Attributes.html#index-nonnull

I will need to send a patch to fix the other links.

> + * clang: https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#nonnull

I think this link goes to `_Nonnull` -- the GNU one is instead:

  https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#id10

(I don't love the numeric IDs, though, since they break, so I think it
is fine either way -- the `_Nonnull` is fairly close to the one we
want and I hope that one doesn't break)

> + */
> +#define __nonnull(x...)                        __attribute__((__nonnull__(x)))

This is indeed available for a long time, and we already use it
elsewhere in the kernel tree (which would be nice to clean up
separately).

If you don't mind, please place it before `__nonstring__` (the file is
meant to be sorted by the actual attribute name -- there are a few
instances where this is not the case anymore, which I will eventually
clean up)

Thanks!

Cheers,
Miguel

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and provided an Acked-by tag after Dmitry addressed his concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from using DEFINE_GUARD() to a class-based approach, which will eventually require non-null arguments due to __nonnull() constructors.

review_comments.author: Dave Jiang
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating no major issues found.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/18/26 8:21 AM, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> The nvdimm_bus guard accepts NULL and skips locking when NULL is passed.
> Convert from DEFINE_GUARD() to DEFINE_CLASS() + DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD().
> 
> This is a preparatory change for making DEFINE_GUARD() constructors
> __nonnull(). nvdimm_bus legitimately passes NULL, so it must be adjusted
> to avoid a compile error.
> 
> No functional change.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

Reviewed-by: Dave Jiang <dave.jiang@intel.com>

> ---
>  drivers/nvdimm/nd.h | 7 +++++--
>  1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/drivers/nvdimm/nd.h b/drivers/nvdimm/nd.h
> index b199eea3260e..18b64559664b 100644
> --- a/drivers/nvdimm/nd.h
> +++ b/drivers/nvdimm/nd.h
> @@ -632,8 +632,11 @@ u64 nd_region_interleave_set_cookie(struct nd_region *nd_region,
>  u64 nd_region_interleave_set_altcookie(struct nd_region *nd_region);
>  void nvdimm_bus_lock(struct device *dev);
>  void nvdimm_bus_unlock(struct device *dev);
> -DEFINE_GUARD(nvdimm_bus, struct device *,
> -	     if (_T) nvdimm_bus_lock(_T), if (_T) nvdimm_bus_unlock(_T));
> +DEFINE_CLASS(nvdimm_bus, struct device *,
> +	     if (_T) nvdimm_bus_unlock(_T),
> +	     ({ if (_T) nvdimm_bus_lock(_T); _T; }),
> +	     struct device *_T);
> +DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(nvdimm_bus);
>  
>  bool is_nvdimm_bus_locked(struct device *dev);
>  void nvdimm_check_and_set_ro(struct gendisk *disk);

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to annotate guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull() attributes, ensuring that the compiler warns when NULL is statically known to be passed. The changes involve adding __nonnull() annotations to unconditional guard constructors and updating documentation links.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the wording of the patch description and suggested rephrasing to accurately reflect the compiler's behavior. Also pointed out outdated documentation links and requested corrections.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 5:22 PM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> Add __nonnull() to unconditional guard constructors so the compiler
> verifies at each call site that NULL is never passed:

> This provides automated, compiler-enforced verification that no
> unconditional guard constructor receives NULL.

I wouldn't say "verify", since the compiler does a best-effort here
with the information it has statically.

In other words, the attribute does not prevent NULL pointers to be passed.

> + *   gcc: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Function-Attributes.html#index-nonnull-function-attribute

Hmm... It appears GCC has changed the docs in commit 6e3c137f5dbb
("doc: Merge function, variable, type, and statement attribute
sections [PR88472]"), dropping the per-kind attribute pages.

So the right link would need to be now:

  https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Attributes.html#index-nonnull

I will need to send a patch to fix the other links.

> + * clang: https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#nonnull

I think this link goes to `_Nonnull` -- the GNU one is instead:

  https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#id10

(I don't love the numeric IDs, though, since they break, so I think it
is fine either way -- the `_Nonnull` is fairly close to the one we
want and I hope that one doesn't break)

> + */
> +#define __nonnull(x...)                        __attribute__((__nonnull__(x)))

This is indeed available for a long time, and we already use it
elsewhere in the kernel tree (which would be nice to clean up
separately).

If you don't mind, please place it before `__nonstring__` (the file is
meant to be sorted by the actual attribute name -- there are a few
instances where this is not the case anymore, which I will eventually
clean up)

Thanks!

Cheers,
Miguel

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda

On Tue, May 19, 2026 at 1:54 PM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> Thanks, fixed locally.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch aims to fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations, specifically addressing the behavior of GFP_ATOMIC and GFP_NORETRY flags.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns that GFP_NORETRY can reclaim and compact once, but ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is still a higher bar. Suggested checking for __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM to decide between fragmenting and failing.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 19, 2026 at 01:47:52PM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 01:24:22PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > On Mon, 18 May 2026 16:37:36 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
> > 
> > > When defrag_mode is enabled, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is enforced to prevent
> > > migratetype fallbacks and keep pageblocks clean. The allocator relies on
> > > reclaim and compaction to free pages of the correct type before allowing
> > > fallback as a last resort.
> > > 
> > > However, non-reclaimable allocations such as GFP_ATOMIC cannot invoke
> > > direct reclaim or compaction. With defrag_mode=1, these allocations hit
> > > the !can_direct_reclaim bailout in __alloc_pages_slowpath() with
> > > ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT still set, and fail without ever attempting a fallback.
> > > 
> > > This causes a large number of SLUB allocation failures for
> > > skbuff_head_cache under network-heavy workloads, despite free memory
> > > being available in other migratetype freelists.
> > > 
> > > Clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry before giving up on allocations that
> > > cannot reclaim, following the same pattern used after reclaim/compaction
> > > exhaustion later in the slowpath.
> > 
> > Thanks.  Sashiko asked a couple of things:
> > 
> > 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260518163736.173910-1-d@ilvokhin.com
> > 
> > I'm not sure what to make of the first one - we aren't holding any locks
> > in there which prevent concurrent cpuset or zonelist alterations
> > anyway (?).
> > 
> > But your change might violate the later comment `No "goto retry;" can be
> > placed above this check * unless it can execute just once'?
> 
> Thanks for taking a look, Andrew.
> 
> Goto retry can execute at most once, since ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is cleared
> before the jump, so on the next iteration the condition is false and we
> fall through to goto nopage. This is the similar to the existing
> can_retry_reserves path.

Yes, it's just a one-off retry with relaxed fragmentation rules, no
need to re-evaluate the cpuset. So this looks fine to me.

> Just for the sake of keeping everything in one place. Another point
> Sashiko raised.
> 
> "Will allocations hitting this PF_MEMALLOC check, or the __GFP_NORETRY check
> further down in the function, still fail prematurely under defrag_mode=1?
> Because these terminal error paths also jump directly to the nopage label,
> they skip the normal ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT clearing at the bottom of the slowpath.
> Should we also clear ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT and retry for these paths so they are
> allowed to fall back rather than failing outright?"
> 
> I think by the time we reach the PF_MEMALLOC check, ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT has
> already been cleared, since we set only ALLOC_NO_WATERMARKS and
> ALLOC_KSWAPD in reserve_flags, when PF_MEMALLOC is set.

Yes, that's correct. alloc_flags gets overwritten, losing NOFRAGMENT,
for privileged requests. And then we retry with that already.

> For GFP_NORETRY, we can do direct reclaim (compared to GFP_ATOMIC case),
> so we either succeed or not, we don't need another round.

This is an interesting question.

GFP_NORETRY can reclaim and compact once, yes, but ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT is
still a higher bar, increasing the likelihood of failure.

However, unlike GFP_ATOMIC, GFP_NORETRY are usually speculative
allocations with reasonable fallback options (like slub's optimistic
higher order requests).

The idea behind defrag_mode is to not fragment until the alternative
is OOM. For GFP_ATOMIC, failing is an OOM-like event. For the other
nopage cases, it's more about "my favorite thing isn't available".

So I'd say let's fix GFP_ATOMIC and leave the other cases alone unless
somebody specifically brings it up as an issue.

However, there is one catch: GFP_ATOMIC is not its own flag. You're
gating on can_direct_reclaim which is also true for optimistic things
like mTHP allocations (GFP_TRANSHUGE_LIGHT e.g.). We don't want to
fragment for those, either.

So I think you'd want to check at least if __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM is set
(which it is for GFP_ATOMIC) to decide between fragmenting and
failing. If the caller doesn't even set that, it's a good indicator
that they're purely speculative, and failing is the better option.

With that,

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] ansible: fix bare conditionals for ansible-core 2.20+ strict booleans
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes issues in Ansible playbooks caused by changes in ansible-core 2.20+. Specifically, it addresses bare conditionals that evaluate to strings instead of booleans, which previously resulted in deprecation warnings but now cause fatal errors. The patch updates various role files to use the correct boolean filters and patterns to handle string variables. This ensures that Ansible playbooks continue to function correctly with the updated core version.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] gitr: enable verbose logging in the test harness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables verbose logging in the test harness of the gitr suite, making it easier to identify what went wrong when tests fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] nfsd: use more reasonable defaults for local VM testing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the default settings for NFS server (nfsd) configuration in Linux kernel, specifically for local VM testing. The changes include increasing the number of threads from 8 to 128 and reducing the lease time and grace period from 90 seconds to 30 seconds. Additionally, it enables version 4.0 support, sets mountd to manage group IDs, and configures dynamic threading by default on newer kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] devconfig: add some extra packages to the kdev tools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds additional packages to the kdev tools installation process, specifically for Red Hat configurations. The new packages include strace, lsof, git, wireshark-cli, rsync, and time, which are useful for development and debugging tasks. This change is part of a larger effort to enhance the devconfig toolset.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues introduced by Ansible version 2.20, which no longer evaluates strings as booleans and instead throws an exception. The patches also make some optional changes to existing workflows, including enabling verbose logging in the test harness and adjusting default settings for local VM testing with NFSd.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] fuse: re-lock request before replacing page cache folio

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] fuse: re-lock request before replacing page cache folio

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] fuse: re-lock request before returning from fuse_ref_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free issue in the fuse kernel module by re-locking the request before returning from the fuse_ref_folio() function, which unlocks the request but does not re-lock it. This ensures that the subsequent copy chain logic can safely access the request's arguments without encountering memory corruption issues. The fix is applied to the fs/fuse/dev.c file and addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch (c3021629a0d8) that added support for splice() reading from fuse devices.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free issue in the FUSE kernel module by re-locking the request before replacing the page cache folio. The problem occurred because fuse_try_move_folio() unlocked the request on entry but did not re-lock it on the success path, allowing the request to be freed while subsequent logic accessed its contents. The patch ensures that the request is locked on the success path by calling lock_request() before replace_page_cache_folio(). This prevents the use-after-free issue and also ensures that the ap->folios[i]->mapping is never null.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 00/21] fuse: extend passthrough to inode operations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 00/21] fuse: extend passthrough to inode operations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/21] fuse: introduce FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO mode, which enforces a one-to-one mapping between fuse inodes and backing file inode numbers. This mode is only supported on 64-bit architectures where the inode number is a u64 type. It allows the kernel to instantiate fuse inodes by passthrough lookup and readdirplus, and notifies the server about these inodes using their backing file inode numbers as unique identifiers. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to support this new mode, including fs/fuse/file.c, fs/fuse/fuse_i.h, fs/fuse/inode.c, and include/uapi/linux/fuse.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/21] fuse: prepare for passthrough of inode operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the fuse filesystem to pass through inode operations by adding a new field 'ops_mask' to the FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN ioctl input argument. The ops_mask declares which inode operations will be passed through to the backing file once it's attached to the fuse inode on lookup. Currently, only regular files are allowed for read/write/splice/mmap passthrough, but this patch relaxes that restriction by allowing non-regular backing files when setting operations other than FUSE_READ/FUSE_WRITE in ops_mask. Additionally, a filesystem with FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is required to set up passthrough of any inode operations for simplification of inode attribute caching.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author suggests decoupling passthrough operation codes from fuse opcode definitions, proposing a new approach where passthrough operations are defined as capabilities rather than specific opcodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do you think we should couple the passthrough op code to the fuse
opcode so closely, instead of defining the passthrough ops separately,
eg doing something like this?:

#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_READ         (1 << 0)
#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_WRITE       (1 << 1)

In the (far) future when some more advanced passthrough features get
added (eg full subtree passthrough), it seems like we'd want to add a
passthrough op for that, but that wouldn't map to a fuse op. I think
there are also some fuse ops we might skip defining as passthrough ops
but are implicitly passed through (eg FUSE_STATX which is covered by
FUSE_GETATTR), so it seems more intuitive to define the passthrough
ops as describing capabilities rather than implying that it describes
what specific opcodes get passed through?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein requested that the _OP_ suffix not be dropped, citing the need to distinguish it from other constants and potentially using a larger data type for ops_mask.

Reviewer questioned the duplication of information between the flags field and the ops_mask field in the fuse_backing_map structure, suggesting it is unnecessary to include flags in ops_mask.

Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the ops_mask field in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN ioctl could also be used for other operations like FUSE_CREATE and FUSE_CREAT, and suggested leaving it as a u64 to avoid potential issues with reserved space.

The reviewer suggested replacing the hardcoded arithmetic in UAPI with a macro-based approach, providing examples of how to use macros for ease of use and suggesting that the current implementation is limiting.
review_comments.raw_body:
Don't drop the _OP_ please
Need it to distinguish from FUSE_PASSTHROUGH{,_INO}
and we need to think if we want to format this as 32bit and extend later
or start with a u64 ops_mask format from the start.

---

I don't know. fuse_backing_map has flags, why would we want to put that
in ops_mask.

---

This could also become the case with FUSE_CREATE{,_HANDLE}
or {FUSE,FUSEX}_CREAT.

TBH, the fact that some ops will never be in the mask and
that some ops have a canonical bit in itself does not justify creating
a different mapping. Do we also want to squeeze the mask into 32bit
and leave reserved space? my intuition is to leave it u64.

---

hmm. I guess you have a point that it is a bit limiting to commit
to this arithmetic in UAPI.

My thinking behind this was ease of use with these helpers:

       if (!fuse_passthrough_op(file_inode(in), FUSE_READ))

but those could just as well be macros:

static inline bool fuse_inode_passthrough_op(struct inode *inode,
                                            u64 opbit)...

#define FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(inode, opname)    \
             fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_ ## opname)

#define FUSE_BACKING_MAP_OP(map, opname) \
       ((map)->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP ## opname))

...
       if (!FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP(file_inode(in), READ))

wdyt?

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees with the macro idea and suggests using u32 for ops_mask, but is open to reviewer's preference.
sentiment_signals: happy to go with what you think
review_comments.raw_body:
Nice, the macro idea is neat!

I feel like it gives more flexibility to define it separately and I
think with that, u32 would be more than enough bits as well. But I'm
happy to go with what you think would be better here.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein expressed uncertainty about changing the type of ops_mask from u64 to something else, suggesting that it's not a pressing concern since it's in an ioctl and could be reconsidered if backing_map responses are moved inline in lookup/readdirplus response.
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure. Let's keep it u64 for now.
Since the struct is in an ioctl, it does not really matter much.
If we ever consider moving backing_map responses inline
in lookup/readdirplus response (maybe over io uring) we could reconsider.

In case you missed the exciting conversations about this API
original fuse passthough patches from Android had the backing fd
in the open response but it was deemed unsafe writing fds to
/dev/fuse, so ioctl was chosen instead [1].

With io_uring I don't think the security concern holds, so I see no reason
why backing_map would not be possible in-place for readdirplus
responses if we wanted to do that, but we can also ignore all this for now.

Thanks,
Amir.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/CAG48ez3ZX8R9kRAQhung2_e3wjowu5cPh7WL3U866mkga-kftQ@mail.gmail.com/

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agreed to modify the ops_mask field in v3, keeping it as a u64 but defining separate constants and using a macro helper.

Author responded to feedback about potential security vulnerability by acknowledging it as a 'clever attack' and expressing gratitude for being informed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sounds good. For v3 I'll keep it a u64 but define them separately and
use your macro helper for mapping from opcode to passthrough op.

---

Wow, that's a clever attack. Thanks for sharing the link.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/21] fuse: prepare for readdir passthrough on directories
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module to support readdir passthrough on directories. It allows directory inodes to have their inode iomode state applicable, and modifies the fuse_sync_release() helper function to handle directories. The changes enable cached mode, direct uncached readdir mode, and readdir passthrough mode for directories, but do not require waiting for parallel dio like regular files. This patch is a step towards implementing readdir passthrough on directories, which will improve performance by reducing the number of system calls required.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/21] fuse: implement passthrough for readdir
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements passthrough for readdir operations in the FUSE file system. It allows a directory to be opened with both cached readdir and readdir passthrough enabled, but not at the same time as regular files. The opt-in for passthrough of READDIR operation means that there is no READDIRPLUS call to the server. This patch also includes changes to ensure that directories opened without both FOPEN_CACHE_DIR and FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH flags are marked as FOPEN_DIRECT_IO, which does not affect io mode.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/21] fuse: prepare for long lived reference on backing file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code to support a long-lived reference to the backing file of an inode, which will be used for passthrough operations and detached when the inode is evicted. The changes include modifications to the fuse_i.h header file, the inode.c file, and the iomode.c file to accommodate this new feature. This patch is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the FUSE code's handling of backing files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/21] fuse: implement passthrough for getattr/statx
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements passthrough functionality for the getattr and statx operations in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module. The patch allows the FUSE driver to forward these requests directly to the underlying filesystem, rather than processing them itself. This is done by calling vfs_getattr() on the backing inode to retrieve the requested attributes, which are then used to update the fuse inode attributes. The patch also introduces a new function, fuse_kstat_to_attr(), that converts a kstat structure into a fuse attribute structure. The passthrough functionality is enabled for getattr and statx operations by setting the FUSE_GETATTR flag in the fuse inode's flags. This approach simplifies the implementation of these operations and reduces the overhead associated with attribute caching.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that fuse_kstat_to_attr() does not handle the case where stat->size is -1, which can lead to incorrect attribute updates.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think you've corrected enough of my flop in this patch to justify
Signed-of-by, but as you wish :)

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/21] fuse: prepare to setup backing inode passthrough on lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a helper function to associate a backing inode for inode passthrough operations in the FUSE file system. The helper, fuse_backing_id_get, retrieves a fuse backing object by its ID and increments its reference count. This is done to prepare for setting up backing inode passthrough on lookup responses. The minimal requirement for the backing inode is to support GETATTR passthrough operations. Unlike open-time setup, this mapping is created for the entire lifetime of the inode and cannot be changed later.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/21] fuse: handle zero ops_mask in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various issues in the FUSE file system, including handling zero ops_mask in FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN to maintain backwards compatibility with older servers.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag without any specific technical comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, May 16, 2026 at 2:52 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Servers that pre-date the ops_mask field will pass a zero ops_mask in
> FUSE_DEV_IOC_BACKING_OPEN. Default this to FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS to
> maintain backwards compatibility.
>
> For FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO servers, an ops_mask must be set.
> FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is a new feature with no backwards compatibliity
> requirements.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

> ---
>  fs/fuse/backing.c | 20 +++++++++++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/backing.c b/fs/fuse/backing.c
> index 2234ab47406a..b499860e1185 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/backing.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/backing.c
> @@ -119,9 +119,23 @@ int fuse_backing_open(struct fuse_conn *fc, struct fuse_backing_map *map)
>         if (map->flags || map->ops_mask & ~FUSE_BACKING_MAP_VALID_OPS)
>                 goto out;
>
> -       /* For now passthrough inode operations requires FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO */
> -       if (!fc->passthrough_ino && map->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INODE_OPS)
> -               goto out;
> +       if (!fc->passthrough_ino) {
> +               /*
> +                * For now passthrough inode operations requires
> +                * FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO
> +                */
> +               if (map->ops_mask & FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INODE_OPS)
> +                       goto out;
> +               /*
> +                * To maintain backwards compatibility with servers that
> +                * pre-date the ops_mask field, a zero ops_mask defaults
> +                * to passing through both reads and writes
> +                */
> +               if (!map->ops_mask)
> +                       map->ops_mask |= FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS;
> +       } else if (!map->ops_mask) {
> +               return -EINVAL;
> +       }
>
>         file = fget_raw(map->fd);
>         res = -EBADF;
> --
> 2.52.0
>
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/21] fuse: handle partial io passthrough for read/write, splice, and mmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows servers to pass through only reads or writes instead of both, avoiding cache coherency issues by separating the backing file's page cache from the fuse inode's page cache. It also rejects mmap operations when passthrough is partial or not set for read/writes since mmap requires both read and write access. The patch includes self-contained fallback logic for handling direct io when passthrough is not enabled.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein expressed discomfort with the removal of fuse_file_passthrough(ff) and suggested keeping the logic contained in a helper function for better documentation.

Reviewer Amir Goldstein suggested using the fuse_file_passthrough_op() helper in the call sites, which would simplify the code and eliminate the need for 'jumping back to direct io' in passthrough.c.

Reviewer Amir Goldstein suggested a more elegant way to handle the existing ENODEV case by changing the helper in the call site, which already covers this scenario.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the fuse_inode_backing() function may not be initialized when the backing inode is set on open, and suggested initializing it to a struct fuse_backing pointer named fb

Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the condition for returning -ENODEV in fuse_passthrough_mmap() is unclear, as it does not account for cases where only GETATTR passthrough is enabled, and suggested relaxing the condition to check for FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This needs to be:
         struct fuse_backing *fb = fuse_inode_backing(fi);

so the helper will work correctly whether the backing inode was set on
lookup (long lived) or on open.

---

This could technically be relaxed to:
          else if (fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS))
                 return -ENODEV;

However, I should note about this sketch patch that the condition for
fuse_passthrough_mmap()
is that both read/write are passthrough and the condition for ENODEV
is that either read/write are passthrough but looking at this code it is
not at all clear to the reader.

Because if there is no read/write passthrough, (only GETATTR passthrough)
there is not really a reason to prevent fuse page cache mmap.

See similar comment I made regarding fuse_file_cached_io_open() on the
documentation patch.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agreed to restructure the code in v3 based on reviewer's suggestions for improvement.

Author agrees that the patch looks cleaner and does not address any specific technical concerns or feedback from Amir Goldstein.
review_comments.raw_body:
I really like all your suggestions, Amir. I agree that this looks much
nicer. I'll incorporate this into v3.

---

Nice! This looks a lot cleaner.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/21] fuse: prepare to cache statx attributes from entry replies
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the fuse kernel module to cache statx attributes from entry replies by adding a new argument to the fuse_iget function, which will store the statx information in the inode's cache. The change is made in preparation for a future patch that will add a new struct fuse_entry2_out outarg containing filled-out statx information returned by the server on LOOKUP/MKDIR/MKNOD requests. Currently, all callers pass NULL as the argument, but this will be updated to use the new statx information once it is available.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/21] fuse: clean up fuse_dentry_revalidate()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch cleans up the fuse_dentry_revalidate function by removing gotos with direct returns and flattening a nested if/else structure, resulting in improved code readability without any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/21] fuse: add struct fuse_entry2_out and helpers for extended entry replies
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new extended entry reply structure called fuse_entry2_out, which carries additional information such as backing_id and statx attributes. It also introduces helpers to process this new structure for passthrough support in lookup, revalidate, and create operations. The existing fuse_statx_to_attr function is moved earlier in the file to avoid forward declarations.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein questioned whether a compound response for LOOKUP+STATX would be beneficial, suggesting it could simplify code and improve handling of backing_id. He also mentioned a test patch on his fuse-backing-inode-wip branch that demonstrates setting backing inode passthrough on getattr.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think you had a discussion with Miklos about whether
it makes sense to do a compound response for LOOKUP+STATX.
I don't remember the conclusion, but if this can save us all the
if (use_entry2) { } else {} all over the code, then I think it is worth it.

Can you give me the bottom line from your discussion or was there
no conclusion reached?

If we do that, where do we return backing_id?
Could make use of the reserved[] fields in fuse_statx_out.

I happen to have a test patch at the tip of my fuse-backing-inode-wip
branch:
6e296625eafd6 fuse: support setting backing inode passthrough on getattr

It's not using the statx response, and it was just done for testing,
but in theory,
if a server wants to  change an inode from non-passthrough to
yes-passthrough mid lifetime
doing this on attribute cache expiry may make more sense than on entry
timeout expiry, because backing_id is an attribute of the inode not the dentry.

Not sure if this is making any sense.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Horst Birthelmer
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Horst Birthelmer expressed uncertainty and lack of clarity regarding the use of compounds in FUSE, specifically whether they will be limited to fusex or also applied to classic FUSE, and apologized for not having a tested patch ready.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Amir,

I see there is a lot of talk about using the compounds in a couple of different contexts.
I really have to apologize for not having the latest patch ready by now, but I'm 
a bit unsure how to do it.

I'm not entirely sure if compounds will only be a thing for fusex or if it will ever
be accepted for the classic FUSE.

I actually have a patch that I have not tested well enough, since I have to maintain
our own version and then have to do the fusex one and probably the new verison for 
the classic FUSE version.

So, sorry about that, but I'm not having enough clarity on what to focus on.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a concern about how to handle fuse and fusex in the patch, but expresses uncertainty about Miklos' thoughts on the matter.

Author agrees that supporting yes passthrough -> no passthrough would require an ioctl mechanism and proposes consistent interfaces for both directions, but acknowledges the need for branching logic to handle new slimmed-down-lookup differently from old lookup.

Author expressed uncertainty about a future plan, indicating that further discussion or clarification is needed to resolve the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the conclusion was that for fusex it will be compounded, but
I'm not sure what Miklos is thinking for fuse.

---

I like this idea but I think for supporting yes passthrough -> no
passthrough, it would have to go through an ioctl mechanism since the
getattr could be passed through. In that case, I was thinking it would
be better to have the interfaces be consistent (eg both yes
passthrough -> no passthrough and no passthrough -> yes passthrough go
through an ioctl interface).

I think even with the backing id as part of statx and lookup+statx
compounded together, there would still have to be the "if (use_entry2)
{} else {}" branching logic to handle the new slimmed-down-lookup
differently from old lookup).

---

I'm not sure what the plan is either. I think this will get clarified
on Thursday hopefully.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein expressed confusion about whether the patch introduces a new FUSE call, indicating he was not aware of it and jokingly mentioned not receiving an invitation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Wait, is there a FUSE call? I did not get an invite :-D

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Luis Henriques
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that fuse_lookup_init() function does not handle the case where both fuse_entry_out and fuse_entry2_out are used simultaneously, which could lead to a memory leak due to double memset

reviewer expressed confusion about the handling of compound and file handles in fusex, citing a discrepancy between the original plan and the current implementation

Reviewer Luis Henriques made a lighthearted comment about not receiving an invitation to something, and did not provide any technical feedback on the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
(Sorry, I'm slow catching-up with email :-) )

On Tue, May 19 2026, Amir Goldstein wrote:

---

thought the plan was clear: both compound and file handles are going to be
in fusex.  Quoting:

---

Don't worry, I think no one has yet received an invite ;-)

Cheers,
-- 
Luís

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that there's uncertainty about whether the new struct fuse_entry2_out and helpers should be added to both fuse and fusex, but no clear plan is stated.

Author responded to a question about scheduling a meeting, stating that only the time was agreed upon and logistics are still being set up by John.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the part we're unclear about is whether the plan is to also
have this in fuse or just fusex.

---

I haven't either. I think the only thing we nailed down was the time
(Thursday at 20:30 CEDT) but I think John is still setting up the
logistics for it.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/21] fuse: add passthrough lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds passthrough lookup support to the FUSE file system. When a backing ID is set for a fuse inode, it associates the inode with the corresponding backing inode for passthrough operations. If no backing ID is set, it caches statx attributes from the reply. The changes are made in the fs/fuse/dir.c file and involve modifications to the fuse_process_entry2 function and the fuse_dentry_revalidate function.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer requested further review of three related lookup patches before providing a detailed assessment.
review_comments.raw_body:
The 3 lookup patches look fine in general, but I will review them more
closely after I have a clear understanding on the desired protocol.

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 14/21] fuse: add passthrough support for entry creation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for passthrough operations in the FUSE file system, specifically for entry creation operations such as mknod, mkdir, symlink, and link. When FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is enabled, it uses the new extended fuse_entry2_out reply to associate a newly created inode with a backing file for passthrough operations. If no backing id is returned, statx attributes are cached instead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 15/21] fuse: add passthrough support for create+open
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds passthrough support for create and open operations in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver. When FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO is enabled, it uses the extended fuse_entry2_out reply to associate a newly created inode with a backing file for passthrough operations before instantiating the dentry. This allows for more efficient handling of create and open requests by reusing existing resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 16/21] fuse: allow backing_id=0 in open to inherit inode's backing file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the FUSE server to pass in a backing ID of 0 when opening a file, which enables the kernel to inherit the inode's existing backing file.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it meets code quality standards.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 17/21] backing-inode: add backing_inode_copyattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic function, backing_inode_copyattr(), to the Linux kernel's fs/backing-inode.c file. The function copies inode attributes from a backing inode to a new inode, taking into account idmappings if present. This allows other filesystems that use backing inodes (such as fuse passthrough) to reuse this logic. The patch also moves the relevant code from overlayfs/util.c to the new generic function.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the patch is mostly good but suggested adding two files to the MAINTAINERS file, specifically the FILESYSTEMS [STACKABLE] section, and also mentioned a potential cleanup patch to remove unused functions d_backing_inode() and backing_inode_security{,_novalidate}(), which he offered to take care of if the submitter doesn't want to deal with it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nice!

Please add these files to
FILESYSTEMS [STACKABLE]
section in MAINTAINERS.

Otherwise, feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

There is one more cleanup patch required, now or later, to get rid of
some awkwardness in kernel fs code.
If you don't want to deal with it, I can take this cleanup later.

d_backing_inode() is a noop from the day it was added and has 177
call sites. Those call sites should be converted to d_inode() and
d_backing_inode() should go away.

backing_inode_security{,_novalidate}() are likewise unneeded
dups of inode_security{,_novalidate}() and should be removed.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees to restructure the code by moving the fs/stack.c logic into ecryptfs, eliminating the need for fs_stack.h
review_comments.raw_body:
Awesome! I'm happy to remove these.

You mentioned in v1 that fs/stack.c and the corresponding
include/linux/fs_stack.h are pretty much only used by ecryptfs. It
looks like there are only two pretty minimal functions in there. Do
you think it's also worth moving that logic into ecryptfs and
eliminating fs stack?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that adding backing_inode_copyattr() would require maintenance from ecryptfs, which is no longer actively maintained and has a maintainer who does not accept changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I wanted to do this once, but ecryptfs is not actively maintained
(odd fixes only), so the maintainer did not want to take any changes.
Let's leave it.

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/21] backing-inode: add backing_inode_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic function called backing_inode_setattr() to handle setattr operations on backing inodes. The function is responsible for updating the attributes of the underlying inode, while also handling locking and write access. This allows other filesystems that use backing inodes (such as fuse passthrough) to reuse this logic. The patch moves the existing ovl_setattr() function's logic into this new generic function, making it easier to maintain and extend.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer expressed skepticism about the quality of the code, referencing past issues in ovl_setattr(), and requested no formal review
review_comments.raw_body:
Wow this code brings back memories that I tried hard to forget :)
I hope that fuse passthrough can reap the benefits of all the bugs
and fixes in ovl_setattr()...

Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 19/21] fuse: add passthrough setattr
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a passthrough setattr feature to the FUSE file system, which allows setting attributes directly on the backing inode instead of sending a FUSE_SETATTR request to the server. This is done by checking for passthrough setattr before stripping suid/sgid permissions in the handle_killpriv/handle_killpriv_v2 functions. The patch introduces new functions and macros to support this feature, including fuse_passthrough_setattr and FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_SETATTR.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged a suggestion to gate FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO on FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2, but disagreed that it was necessary due to calling fuse_passthrough_setattr() in the wrong place. Instead, she proposed having the backing filesystem handle suid/sgid stripping atomically through its own notify_change() path.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the v1 discussion [1], there was a suggestion about gating
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO on FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 to avoid the
non-atomic killpriv path, but I think we were only encountering the
non-atomic killpriv path because I was calling
fuse_passthrough_setattr() in the wrong place. I think we can avoid
this altogether by just having the backing filesystem handle all of
the suid/sgid stripping atomically through its own notify_change()
path, which will let us skip all the fuse killpriv handling logic in
this function.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/CAOQ4uxipQJt5zskb_THxueGi_MXpFdywFiGpd_nmWeY_sMHwzQ@mail.gmail.com/

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein questioned the necessity of a separate patch for handling FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO and FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2, suggesting that if the server requires privilge removal, it will likely apply attribute changes to the backing inode, making FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 the natural choice. He also pointed out that a similar logic applies to ATOMIC_O_TRUNC.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think it still matters, but to the only-passthrough-GETATTR case
and I am still in favor of making this change.
My point was why have the discussion whether getattr
for the purpose of killpriv should be passthrough or not (next patch)?
Why not drop the next patch instead because it will become moot.

If FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO requires FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2
it means that FUSE kernel is never trying to play games removing privs
itself, not with passthrough and not without passthrough.

Quoting your comment on next patch
"I think there might be some use cases
though where having only getattr passed through and not setattr is
useful (eg server wants fast stat() but needs to intercept attribute
changes for access control or policy enforcement)."

Valid use case, but in this valid use case, said server may reject/allow
attribute changes, but eventually if it allows them it will likely apply
them to the very file which was set as backing inode, so essentially
FUSE_HANDLE_KILLPRIV_V2 is the natural choice for a
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO server, even when it wants to introspect
setattr.

I think the same is true for ATOMIC_O_TRUNC.
Either backing fs deals with O_TRUNC or server deals with O_TRUNC.

But this is my opinion - I am open to hearing other opinions.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that handling KILLPRIV_V2 stripping in the server is not burdensome, agreed to require FUSE_KILLPRIV_V2 for passthrough inode operations in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
I was thinking from the user perspective, it'd be nicer to not couple
things unless it was absolutely necessary, especially since
passthrough is a per-inode/per-file attribute whereas KILLPRIV_V2 is a
global server configuration (eg server might want passthrough on just
a few files without taking on killpriv responsibility for the whole
filesystem).

But I think everything you wrote makes sense and I don't think
handling the stripping stuff is too burdensome to ask of the server.

For v3, I'll make the change to require FUSE_KILLPRIV_V2 for
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 20/21] fuse: use passthrough getattr in setattr suid/sgid handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module to handle suid/sgid permissions when setting attributes on files. The goal is to ensure that these permissions are correctly propagated from the server to the client, even if the getattr operation is passed through.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Raised the possibility that for getattr operations related to killpriv, it may make sense to ask the server's mode for calculating the setattr mode. However, did not express a strong opinion or request changes.

>

> >                          * ia_mode calculation may have used stale i_mode.
> >                          * Refresh and recalculate.
> >                          */
> > -                       ret = fuse_do_getattr(idmap, inode, NULL, file);
> > +                       if (fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_GETATTR))
> > +                               ret = fuse_passthrough_getattr(inode, NULL,
> > +                                                              STATX_MODE, 0);
> > +                       else
> > +                               ret = fuse_do_getattr(idmap, inode, NULL, file);
>
> I left this part untouched from v1 but there was a discussion [1]
> about whether to enforce that setattr must be passed through if
> getattr is passed through [1]. I think there might be some use cases
> though where having only getattr passed through and not setattr is
> useful (eg server wants fast stat() but needs to intercept attribute
> changes for access control or policy enforcement).

Of course there is. I think I was misunderstood.

What I meant was that *specifically* for the getattr for this purpose
of killpriv, it may make sense to ask the server's mode for
calculating the setattr mode for the server.

But this is just a hunch.
I could be wrong and I have no strong feelings either way.

> I'm not sure if you
> still feel it's better to keep it simple with enforcing that

No need.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.summary: Clarified her intention to leave the setattr operation untouched from v1, but expressed uncertainty about whether it's better to enforce that getattr and setattr must always be passed through together.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 15, 2026 at 5:53 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> When refreshing i_mode for suid/sgid kill during setattr, use
> passthrough getattr if the inode has that enabled.

> index b67b3b334e69..fd1b3fd86968 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/dir.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/dir.c
> @@ -2524,7 +2524,11 @@ static int fuse_setattr(struct mnt_idmap *idmap, struct dentry *entry,
>                          * ia_mode calculation may have used stale i_mode.

>                          */
> -                       ret = fuse_do_getattr(idmap, inode, NULL, file);
> +                       if (fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_GETATTR))

I left this part untouched from v1 but there was a discussion [1]

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 21/21] docs: fuse: document extended passthrough (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically about extended passthrough mode (FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INO). This new feature allows for inode operations and directory operations to be passed through from the user space to the kernel, with a one-to-one mapping between fuse inode numbers and backing file inode numbers. The documentation explains how to enable this feature, including steps for negotiating the capability during FUSE_INIT, registering backing files, and handling requests in the daemon.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the documentation incorrectly states that files opened with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO deny entry into both caching and passthrough IOMODE, when in fact they only deny entry into caching.

Reviewer noted that the requirement for FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH when opening files in caching mode without doing IO passthrough could be perplexing to users, and suggested either documenting this limitation or relaxing the restriction.
review_comments.raw_body:
More accurately, no open files in caching or passthough.
files open with FOPEN_DIRECT_IO may exist and they
do not deny entering either caching or passthrough iomode.

---

I had not considered this.
Setting OP_GETATTR/SETATTR in LOOKUP without any OP_RW bits
and then requiring that the server open files with FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH
with the backing_id sounds strange -
I understand why it was done this way, but for users this could be perplexing.

The thing is that if the server is only doing GETATTR passthrough there is not
really a reason to deny open file in caching mode (I think?).

The limitation that requires FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH is encoded in
fuse_file_cached_io_open()
    fuse_is_io_cache_wait()
        !fuse_inode_backing()

We have two options.

Either we decide that server passing through GETATTR and opening
files in caching mode is not interesting to support (for now) and then just
maybe we need to better document the reason that server needs to
use FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH despite not doing io passthrough.

Or we relax the limitation w.r.t cached io mode, something like this
(completely untested most likely broken based or earlier review
suggestion regarding fuse_passthrough_op):

Thanks,
Amir.

diff --git a/fs/fuse/iomode.c b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
index c5f3e2201c6ab..7c33e7a974f6c 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/iomode.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/iomode.c
@@ -20,7 +20,8 @@ static inline bool fuse_is_io_cache_wait(struct inode *inode)

        return S_ISREG(inode->i_mode) &&
-               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 && !fuse_inode_backing(fi);
+               READ_ONCE(fi->iocachectr) < 0 &&
+               !fuse_passthrough_op(inode, FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_RW_OPS);
 }

 /*
@@ -54,7 +55,7 @@ int fuse_file_cached_io_open(struct inode *inode,
struct fuse_file *ff)
         * Check if inode entered passthrough io mode while waiting for parallel

-       if (fuse_inode_backing(fi)) {

                spin_unlock(&fi->lock);
                return -ETXTBSY;
@@ -285,7 +286,8 @@ int fuse_file_io_open(struct file *file, struct
inode *inode)

         */

-       if (fuse_inode_backing(fi) && !(ff->open_flags & FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH))
